questions.questionText:
Consider the relation $R$ on the set $\{-2,-1,0,1,2\}$ defined by $(a, b) \in R$ if and only if $1+a b>0$. Then, among the statements :

I. The number of elements in R is 17

II. R is an equivalence relation
options:
Only I is true

Only II is true

Both I and II are true

Neither I nor II is true
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We first observe the relation $\boldsymbol{R}$ defined on

$\boldsymbol{S} \times \boldsymbol{S} (\boldsymbol{a}, \boldsymbol{b})$ is an ordered pair $\in \boldsymbol{R}$ iff $(1+\boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{b})>0=\boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{b}>-1 \ldots \ldots(\boldsymbol{i})$

Refer the chart below :

a b ab ∈ R
−2 −2 4 Yes
−1 −1 2 Yes
0 0 0 Yes
1 1 2 Yes
2 2 4 Yes
−2 −1 2 Yes
..... ..... ..... Yes
..... ..... ..... Yes

We also observe the mathematical relationship from $(\boldsymbol{i})$ :

$a b>-1 \Rightarrow b a>-1 \equiv(a, b) \in R \Rightarrow(b, a) \in R \ldots \ldots(i i) \leftarrow$ symmetric

We take 2 ordered pair entries from $\boldsymbol{R}$ :

$$ \begin{array}{|r|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Ordered pair }(\boldsymbol{a}, \boldsymbol{b}) & (2,0) & \boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{b}>-1 & (0,-2) & \boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{b}>-1 & (2,-2) & \boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{b} \ngtr-1 & \text { Transitive } \\ \hline \boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{b} & 0 & 0>-1 & 0 & 0>-1 & -4 & -4<-1 & \\ \hline \in \boldsymbol{R} & \text { Yes } & & \text { Yes } & & \text { No } & & \text { No } \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$\therefore $$ $$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text { Relation } & \text { Reflexive } & \text { Symmetric } & \text { Transitive } \\ \hline \boldsymbol{R} & \text { Yes } & \text { Yes } & \text { No } \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Now let us focus on the cardinality of

$\boldsymbol{R} \in \boldsymbol{S} \times \boldsymbol{S} ;(\boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{b}>-1)$

S R a b No of entries Cumulative Total
{ −2, −1, 0, 1, 2 } ab > −1 −2 ∈ { −2, −1, 0 } 3 3
{ −2, −1, 0, 1, 2 } ab > −1 −1 ∈ { −2, −1, 0 } 3 6
{ −2, −1, 0, 1, 2 } ab > −1 0 ∈ S 5 11
{ −2, −1, 0, 1, 2 } ab > −1 1 ∈ { 0, 1, 2 } 3 14
{ −2, −1, 0, 1, 2 } ab > −1 2 ∈ { 0, 1, 2 } 3 17

$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline \mathrm{I} & \text { The number of elements in } \mathrm{R} \text { is } 17 & \text { Valid } & \text { Option } \\ \hline \mathrm{II} & \mathrm{R} \text { is an equivalence relation } & \text { Invalid } & \mathrm{A} \\ \hline \end{array} $$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let $\mathrm{R}=\left\{(x, y) \in \mathbf{N} \times \mathbf{N}: \log _{\mathrm{e}}(x+y) \leq 2\right\}$. Then the minimum number of elements, required to be added in $R$ to make it a transitive relation, is $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We have

$$ R=\{(x,y)\in \mathbb{N}\times \mathbb{N}:\ln(x+y)\le 2\}. $$

To make the relation transitive, we first find all pairs already in $R$.

Since

$$ \ln(x+y)\le 2 \iff x+y\le e^2. $$

Now,

$$ e^2 \approx 7.389. $$

So, for natural numbers $x,y$, we must have

$$ x+y\le 7. $$

Hence,

$$ R=\{(x,y)\in \mathbb{N}\times \mathbb{N}:x+y\le 7\}. $$

Now list all such pairs:

  • For $x=1$: $(1,1),(1,2),(1,3),(1,4),(1,5),(1,6)$

  • For $x=2$: $(2,1),(2,2),(2,3),(2,4),(2,5)$

  • For $x=3$: $(3,1),(3,2),(3,3),(3,4)$

  • For $x=4$: $(4,1),(4,2),(4,3)$

  • For $x=5$: $(5,1),(5,2)$

  • For $x=6$: $(6,1)$

So these are the elements of $R$.

A relation is transitive if whenever

$$ (a,b)\in R \quad \text{and} \quad (b,c)\in R, $$

then

$$ (a,c)\in R. $$

We need to find all pairs $(a,c)$ that must be present because of transitivity.

Step 1: Find which middle elements are possible

If $(a,b)\in R$, then

$$ a+b\le 7. $$

If $(b,c)\in R$, then

$$ b+c\le 7. $$

For a fixed $b$, this gives

$$ a\le 7-b,\qquad c\le 7-b. $$

So for each $b$, transitivity demands that all pairs

$$ (a,c)\quad \text{with } 1\le a\le 7-b,\; 1\le c\le 7-b $$

must be in the relation.

Taking union over all possible $b\in \mathbb{N}$ with at least one such pair, we get:

  • For $b=1$: required pairs are all

    $$ (a,c),\quad 1\le a\le 6,\; 1\le c\le 6. $$

This is already the largest possible set forced by transitivity.

So, if relation is to be transitive, it must contain every pair

$$ (a,c)\in \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}\times \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}. $$

That means the transitive closure must at least contain all $36$ pairs

$$ (a,c),\quad 1\le a,c\le 6. $$

Step 2: Count how many of these are already in $R$

Among these $36$ pairs, $R$ already contains those with

$$ a+c\le 7. $$

Count them:

  • $a=1$: $c=1$ to $6$ $\Rightarrow 6$

  • $a=2$: $c=1$ to $5$ $\Rightarrow 5$

  • $a=3$: $c=1$ to $4$ $\Rightarrow 4$

  • $a=4$: $c=1$ to $3$ $\Rightarrow 3$

  • $a=5$: $c=1$ to $2$ $\Rightarrow 2$

  • $a=6$: $c=1$ $\Rightarrow 1$

Total:

$$ 6+5+4+3+2+1=21. $$

So the number of additional pairs needed is

$$ 36-21=15. $$

Step 3: Check that this is sufficient

If we add all missing pairs among

$$ \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}\times \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}, $$

then the relation becomes

$$ \{(a,c):1\le a\le 6,\;1\le c\le 6\}. $$

This is clearly transitive, because whenever

$$ (a,b)\in R \quad \text{and} \quad (b,c)\in R, $$

all three numbers lie in $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$, so $(a,c)$ is also in the relation.

Therefore, the minimum number of elements to be added is

$$ \boxed{15} $$

questions.questionText: Let $\mathrm{A}=\{1,4,7\}$ and $\mathrm{B}=\{2,3,8\}$. Then the number of elements, in the relation $R=\left\{\left(\left(a_1, b_1\right),\left(a_2, b_2\right)\right) \in((A \times B) \times(A \times B)): a_1+b_2\right.$ divides $\left.a_2+b_1\right\}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We need to count the number of ordered pairs

$$ \left( (a_1,b_1),(a_2,b_2) \right) \in (A\times B)\times (A\times B) $$

such that

$$ a_1+b_2 \text{ divides } a_2+b_1. $$

Given

$$ A=\{1,4,7\}, \qquad B=\{2,3,8\} $$

first note that

$$ A\times B=\{(1,2),(1,3),(1,8),(4,2),(4,3),(4,8),(7,2),(7,3),(7,8)\} $$

So there are $9$ elements in $A\times B$, and hence $9\times 9=81$ ordered pairs in $(A\times B)\times(A\times B)$.

We now count only those for which

$$ a_1+b_2 \mid a_2+b_1. $$

Let

$$ x=a_1+b_2,\qquad y=a_2+b_1. $$

Since $a_1,a_2\in\{1,4,7\}$ and $b_1,b_2\in\{2,3,8\}$, possible values of such sums are:

$$ 1+2=3,\;1+3=4,\;1+8=9, $$

$$ 4+2=6,\;4+3=7,\;4+8=12, $$

$$ 7+2=9,\;7+3=10,\;7+8=15. $$

So possible values are

$$ 3,4,6,7,9,10,12,15. $$

Now we count how many times each value occurs as a sum of one element from $A$ and one from $B$:

  • $3$ occurs once: $(1,2)$

  • $4$ occurs once: $(1,3)$

  • $6$ occurs once: $(4,2)$

  • $7$ occurs once: $(4,3)$

  • $9$ occurs twice: $(1,8),(7,2)$

  • $10$ occurs once: $(7,3)$

  • $12$ occurs once: $(4,8)$

  • $15$ occurs once: $(7,8)$

Let this frequency be $f(s)$.

Now for each possible value of $x=a_1+b_2$, we count possible $y=a_2+b_1$ such that $x\mid y$.

Also, number of ways to get a given $x$ is $f(x)$, and number of ways to get a given $y$ is $f(y)$.

Hence total number required is

$$ \sum_{x\mid y} f(x)f(y) $$

where $x,y$ run over the above sums.

Now check each $x$:

1. For $x=3$

Multiples among the possible sums:

$$ 3,6,9,12,15 $$

So contribution is

$$ f(3)\big(f(3)+f(6)+f(9)+f(12)+f(15)\big) $$

$$ =1(1+1+2+1+1)=6 $$

2. For $x=4$

Multiples among possible sums:

$$ 4,12 $$

Contribution:

$$ 1(1+1)=2 $$

3. For $x=6$

Multiples among possible sums:

$$ 6,12 $$

Contribution:

$$ 1(1+1)=2 $$

4. For $x=7$

Multiples among possible sums:

$$ 7 $$

Contribution:

$$ 1\cdot 1=1 $$

5. For $x=9$

Multiples among possible sums:

$$ 9 $$

Contribution:

$$ 2\cdot 2=4 $$

6. For $x=10$

Multiples among possible sums:

$$ 10 $$

Contribution:

$$ 1\cdot 1=1 $$

7. For $x=12$

Multiples among possible sums:

$$ 12 $$

Contribution:

$$ 1\cdot 1=1 $$

8. For $x=15$

Multiples among possible sums:

$$ 15 $$

Contribution:

$$ 1\cdot 1=1 $$

Now add all contributions:

$$ 6+2+2+1+4+1+1+1=18 $$

Therefore, the number of elements in the relation $R$ is

$$ \boxed{18} $$

questions.questionText: Let $A = \{2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$. Let $R$ be a relation on the set $A \times A$ given by $(x, y)R(z, w)$ if and only if $x$ divides $z$ and $y \leq w$. Then the number of elements in $R$ is _________.
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We are given the set $A = \{2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$.

The relation $R$ is defined on the Cartesian product $A \times A$, meaning its elements are ordered pairs of the form $((x, y), (z, w))$ where all $x, y, z, w \in A$.

The condition for the relation $(x, y) R (z, w)$ is:

  1. $x$ divides $z$

  2. $y \leq w$

Notice that the condition on $x$ and $z$ is completely independent of the condition on $y$ and $w$. Therefore, by the Fundamental Principle of Counting, the total number of elements in $R$ will be the number of possible pairs for $(x, z)$ multiplied by the number of possible pairs for $(y, w)$.

Find the number of pairs $(x, z)$ where $x$ divides $z$

Let us list down the possible values for $z \in A$ for each $x \in A$.

  • If $x = 2$, then $z$ can be $2, 4,$ or $6$. (This gives 3 pairs)

  • If $x = 3$, then $z$ can be $3$ or $6$. (This gives 2 pairs)

  • If $x = 4$, then $z$ can only be $4$. (This gives 1 pair)

  • If $x = 5$, then $z$ can only be $5$. (This gives 1 pair)

  • If $x = 6$, then $z$ can only be $6$. (This gives 1 pair)

Adding these up, the total number of pairs for $(x, z)$ is:

$$3 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 8$$

Find the number of pairs $(y, w)$ where $y \leq w$

Let us list down the possible values for $w \in A$ for each $y \in A$.

  • If $y = 2$, then $w$ can be $2, 3, 4, 5,$ or $6$. (This gives 5 pairs)

  • If $y = 3$, then $w$ can be $3, 4, 5,$ or $6$. (This gives 4 pairs)

  • If $y = 4$, then $w$ can be $4, 5,$ or $6$. (This gives 3 pairs)

  • If $y = 5$, then $w$ can be $5$ or $6$. (This gives 2 pairs)

  • If $y = 6$, then $w$ can only be $6$. (This gives 1 pair)

Adding these up, the total number of pairs for $(y, w)$ is:

$$5 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 15$$

Calculate the total number of elements in $R$

Now, we multiply the number of possibilities for $(x, z)$ and $(y, w)$ together:

$$\text{Total elements in } R = 8 \times 15 = 120$$

Therefore, the number of elements in $R$ is $120$.

questions.questionText:
Let $R$ be a relation defined on the set $\{1,2,3,4\} \times\{1,2,3,4\}$ by

$$ \mathrm{R}=\{((a, b),(c, d)): 2 a+3 b=3 c+4 d\} . $$

Then the number of elements in R is
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
let $A=\{1,2,3,4\}$

$R$ is defined on $A \times A$ such that

$$ \begin{aligned} & R=\{((a, b),(c, d)): 2 a+3 b=3 c+4 d\} \\ & \text { since } a, b, c, d \in A \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { Let possible values of } 2 a+3 b=L 1 \text { and possible values of } 3 c+4 d=L 2 $$

$$ \begin{array}{c|c|c|c} a & b & 2 a+3 b & 3 c+4 d \\ \hline 1 & 1 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & 2 & 8 & 11 \\ 1 & 3 & 11 & 15 \\ 1 & 4 & 14 & 19 \\ 2 & 1 & 7 & 10 \\ 2 & 2 & 10 & 14 \\ 2 & 3 & 13 & 18 \\ 2 & 4 & 16 & 22 \\ 3 & 1 & 9 & 13 \\ 3 & 2 & 12 & 17 \\ 3 & 3 & 15 & 21 \\ 3 & 4 & 18 & 25 \\ 4 & 1 & 11 & 16 \\ 4 & 2 & 14 & 20 \\ 4 & 3 & 17 & 24 \\ 4 & 4 & 20 & 28 \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Common values of } 2 a+3 b=3 c+4 d\\ &7,10,11,13,14,15,16,17,18,20 \end{aligned} $$

value $7,10,13,15,16,17,18,20$

occurs 1 time in L1 and 1 time in L2

e.g value 7 occurs 1 time in L1 $\&$ 1 time in L2 total $=1 \times 1=1$

so, total number of these types of elements

in $\mathrm{R}=8$--- (1)

value 11 and 14 occurs 2 times in L1

and 1 time in L2

e.g value 11 occurs 2 time in L1 $\&$ 1 time in L2 total

elements in $R$ is $2 \times 1=2$

so total number these type of elements $=2+2=4$--- (2)

total number of elements in R = 8+4 = 12
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
Consider two sets $\mathrm{A}=\{x \in \mathrm{Z}:|(|x-3|-3)| \leq 1\}$ and

$\mathrm{B}=\left\{x \in \mathbb{R}-\{1,2\}: \frac{(x-2)(x-4)}{x-1} \log _e(|x-2|)=0\right\}$. Then the number of

onto functions $f: \mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Set } A=\{x \in \mathbb{Z}:|(|x-3|-3)| \leq 1\} \\\\ & |(|x-3|-3)| \leq 1 \quad\{|x| \leq a \Rightarrow x \in[-a, a]\}\end{aligned}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $(|x-3|-3) \in[-1,1]$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $ |x-3| \in[2,4] $

$ \text { Using }|x| \in[\alpha, \beta] \Rightarrow x \in[-\beta,-\alpha] \cup[\alpha, \beta] $

$ x-3 \in[-4,-2] \cup[2,4]$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ x \in[-1,1] \cup[5,7] $$

Since $x \in \mathbb{Z}$ (integers), $x=-1,0,1,5,6,7$

So set $A=\{-1,0,1,5,6,7\}$

Set $B=\left\{x \in \mathbb{R}-\{1,2\}, \frac{(x-2)(x-4)}{(x-1)} \log _e(|x-2|)=0\right\}$

$\frac{(x-2)(x-4)}{(x-1)} \log _e(|x-2|)=0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $x-2=0 \Rightarrow x=2$ but $x \neq 2$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $x-4=0 \Rightarrow x=4$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\log _e|x-2|=0 \Rightarrow|x-2|=1$

$$ \begin{aligned} & x-2= \pm 1 \Rightarrow x=3,1 \text { but } x \neq 1 \\ & \text { So set } B=\{3,4\} \\ & \text { Let } n(A) \& n(B) \text { be number of elements in set } A \text { and } B \\ & \text { respectively } \\ & n(A)=6, n(B)=2 \end{aligned} $$

Number of onto function $f: A \rightarrow B$ is

= Total function - Into function

Total function $=(n(B))^{n(A)}=2^6$

number of Into function can be find using inclusion-exclusion principle

Number of into function $={ }^2 C_1(1)^6$

${ }^2 C_1(1)^6=$ only one image or range left from set $B$.

Number of onto function $=2^6-{ }^2 C_1(1)^6$

$=64-2=62$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
Let $\mathrm{A}=\{0,1,2, \ldots, 9\}$. Let R be a relation on A defined by $(x, y) \in \mathrm{R}$ if and only if $|x-y|$ is a multiple of 3.

Given below are two statements :

Statement I : $n(\mathrm{R})=36$.

Statement II : R is an equivalence relation.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect

Both Statement I and Statement II are correct

Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect

Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$ A=\{0,1,2, \ldots 9\} $$

A relation $R$ on $A$

$R=|x-y|$ is multiple of 3

$0,3,6,9$ are $3 n$ type numbers

$1,4,7$ are $3 n+1$ type numbers

$2,5,8$ are $3 n+2$ type numbers

Case 1 : Relation $R$ is valid if $x \& y$ both are $3 n$ type.

$$ =4 \times 4=16 \text { pairs } $$

Case 2 : Relation $R$ is valid if $x$ is $3 n+1$ type $\& y$ is $3 n+2$ type. $=3 \times 3=9$ pairs

Case 3 : similarly relation $R$ is valid if $x$ is $3 n+2$ type $\& y$ is $3 n+1$ type. $=3 \times 3=9$ pair

$$ n(R)=16+9+9=34 \neq 36 $$

statement I is false.

A relation is an equivalence relation if it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.

Reflexive: For any $x \in A,|x-x|=0$. Since 0 is a multiple of $3,(x, x) \in R$.

Symmetric: If $(x, y) \in R$, then $|x-y|=3 k$. Since $|y-x|=|x-y|$, then $|y-x|= 3 k$, so $(y, x) \in R$.

Transitive: If $|x-y|$ and $|y-z|$ are multiples of 3 , then $x-y=3 k$ and $y-z=3 m$.

Adding these gives $x-z=3(k+m)$, so $|x-z|$ is a multiple of 3.

Statement II is correct.
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let $\mathrm{A}=\{-2,-1,0,1,2,3,4\}$. Let R be a relation on A defined by $x \mathrm{R} y$ if and only if $2 x+y \leqslant 2$. Let $l$ be the number of elements in R . Let m and n be the minimum number of elements required to be added in R to make it reflexive and symmetric relations respectively. Then $\mathrm{l}+\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to :
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \frac{x}{y} & (-2) & (-1) & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ \hline-2 & P & P & P & P & P & A & A \\ \hline-1 & P & P & P & P & A & A & A \\ \hline 0 & P & P & P & P & A & & \\ \hline 1 & P & P & P & A & & & \\ \hline 2 & P & P & P & & A & & \\ \hline 3 & P & P & & & & A & \\ \hline 4 & P & P & & & & & A \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Here $P$ implies, $A$ implies absent

$\Rightarrow \quad 10$ elements to be added as $A=10, P=23$

$\Rightarrow A+P=33$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: The number of elements in the relation $\mathrm{R}=\left\{(x, y): 4 x^2+y^2<52, x, y \in \mathbf{Z}\right\}$ is
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} &\underset{ \,\,\,\,\,\downarrow}{4} x^2+y_{\downarrow}^2<52, x, y \in Z \\ & 0 \quad 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3, \pm 4, \pm 5, \pm 6, \pm 7 \rightarrow 1 \times 15=15 \\ & \pm 1 \quad 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3 \ldots \ldots \ldots . \pm 6 \rightarrow 2 \times 13=26 \\ & +2 \quad 0, \pm 1 \pm 2 \pm 3 \ldots \ldots \ldots . \pm 5 \rightarrow 2 \times 11=22 \\ & \pm 3 \quad 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3 \rightarrow 2 \times 7=14 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Number of elements }=77 \end{aligned} $$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let S be the set of the first 11 natural numbers. Then the number of elements in $A=\{B \subseteq S: n(B) \geqslant 2$ and the product of all elements of $B$ is even $\}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Let $S=\{1,2,\dots,11\}$. The product of all elements of $B$ is even iff $B$ contains at least one even number.

  • Evens in $S$: $5$ ($2,4,6,8,10$)

  • Odds in $S$: $6$

Number of subsets containing at least one even:

$ 2^{11}-2^{6}=2048-64=1984 $

(we subtract the subsets made only of odds).

Now enforce $n(B)\ge 2$: subtract the 5 singleton subsets $\{2\},\{4\},\{6\},\{8\},\{10\}$.

$ 1984-5=1979 $

$ \boxed{1979} $

questions.questionText:
Let the relation R on the set $\mathrm{M}=\{1,2,3, \ldots, 16\}$ be given by $\mathrm{R}=\{(x, y): 4 y=5 x-3, x, y \in \mathrm{M}\}$.

Then the minimum number of elements required to be added in R , in order to make the relation symmetric, is equal to
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & M=\{1,2,3 \ldots .16\} \\ & R=\{(x, y) / 4 y=5 x-3, x, y \in N\} \\ & 4 y=5 x-3 \\ & y=\frac{5 x-3}{4} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { No of order pairs }=4 \end{aligned} $$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: Let $A = \{2, 3, 5, 7, 9\}$. Let $R$ be the relation on $A$ defined by $xRy$ if and only if $2x \leq 3y$. Let $l$ be the number of elements in $R$, and $m$ be the minimum number of elements required to be added in $R$ to make it a symmetric relation. Then $l + m$ is equal to:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
For $x=2$

$$ egin{aligned} & 4 \leq 3 y \quad y \in\{2,3,5,7,9\} \ & (2,2)(2,3)(2,5)(2,7)(2,5) \end{aligned} $$

For $x=3$

$$ egin{aligned} & 6 \leq 3 y y \in\{2,3,5,7,9\} \ & (3,2)(3,3)(3,5)(3,7)(3,5) \end{aligned} $$

For $x=5$

$$ 10 \leq 3 y y \in\{5,7,9\} $$

$$ egin{aligned} & (5,5)(5,7)(5,5) \ & ext { For } x=7 \ & 14 \leq 3 y y \in\{5,7,9\} \ & (7,5)(7,7)(7,9) \ & ext { For } x=9 \ & 18 \leq 3 y y \in\{7,9\} \ & (9,7)(9,9) \ & R=5+5+3+3+2=18 \ & (2,3) \in R ext { but }(3,2) \in R(\operatorname{symmetric}) \ & (2,5) \in R ext { but }(5,2) otin R \operatorname{Add}(5,2) \ & (2,7) \in R ext { but }(7,2) otin R \operatorname{Add}(7,2) \ & (2,9) \in R ext { but }(9,2) otin R \operatorname{Add}(9,2) \ & (3,5) \in R ext { but }(5,3) otin R \operatorname{Add}(5,3) \ & (3,7) \in R ext { but }(7,3) otin R \operatorname{Add}(7,3) \ & (3,9) \in R ext { but }(9,3) otin R \operatorname{Add}(9,3) \ & (5,7) \in R ext { but }(7,5) \in R \ & (5,9) \in R ext { but }(9,5) otin R \operatorname{Add}(9,5) \ & (7,9) \in R ext { but }(9,7) \in R \ & 18+7=25 \end{aligned} $$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText: The number of relations, defined on the set $\{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}\}$, which are both reflexive and symmetric, is equal to:
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Number of such relations:

From $A \rightarrow A$ such that

$$ \begin{aligned} & n(A)=N \text { is } \\ & \Rightarrow 2^{\left(\frac{N^2-N}{2}\right)} \end{aligned} $$

for $N=4 \Rightarrow 2^6=64$

Alter :

$$ \begin{aligned} & {\left[\begin{array}{cccc} 0 & - & - & - \\ \Theta & 0 & - & - \\ \Theta & \Theta & 0 & - \\ \Theta & \Theta & \Theta & 0 \end{array}\right] \Rightarrow \text { each } \Theta \text { has two choices }} \\ & \Rightarrow 2^6=64 \end{aligned} $$
questions.instruction: Choose the correct option out of the four choices.

questions.questionText:
Let A = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5}. Let R be a relation on A defined by (x, y) ∈ R if and only if max{x, y} ∈ {3, 4}. Then among the statements

(S1): The number of elements in R is 18, and

(S2): The relation R is symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive
questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
To evaluate the relation $ R $ on the set $ A = \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} $, we first need to understand the conditions for an element $(x, y)$ to be in $ R $. Specifically, $(x, y) \in R$ if and only if $\max\{x, y\} \in \{3, 4\}$.

Considering this, let's list the pairs:

For $\max\{x, y\} = 3$, the possible pairs are:

$(0, 3), (3, 0), (1, 3), (3, 1), (2, 3), (3, 2), (3, 3)$

For $\max\{x, y\} = 4$, the possible pairs are:

$(0, 4), (4, 0), (1, 4), (4, 1), (2, 4), (4, 2), (3, 4), (4, 3), (4, 4)$

Combining these, the set $ R $ consists of the following elements:

$ R = \{(0, 3), (3, 0), (1, 3), (3, 1), (2, 3), (3, 2), (3, 3), (0, 4), (4, 0), (1, 4), (4, 1), (2, 4), (4, 2), (3, 4), (4, 3), (4, 4)\} $

This gives us a total of 16 elements in $ R $, not 18 as initially claimed in statement $ S_1 $.

Next, we analyze the properties of the relation $ R $:

Reflexivity: A relation is reflexive if $(x, x) \in R$ for all $ x \in A$. For example, $(0, 0), (1, 1), (2, 2)$ are not in $ R $, so $ R $ is not reflexive.

Symmetry: A relation is symmetric if whenever $(a, b) \in R$, then $(b, a) \in R$ as well. For all pairs $(x, y)$ listed, both $(x, y)$ and $(y, x)$ are present. Thus, $ R $ is symmetric.

Transitivity: A relation is transitive if whenever $(a, b) \in R$ and $(b, c) \in R$, then $(a, c) \in R$. An example where transitivity fails is $(0, 3)$ and $(3, 1)$ are in $ R $ but $(0, 1)$ is not in $ R$. Therefore, $ R $ is not transitive.

In conclusion, statement $ S_2 $ is correct as $ R $ is symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive.
